review.summary: The series is not ready as posted. There is one blocking regression in the boot path, one high-risk spec mismatch in page-table-walk endianness selection, and a remaining design hole around per-hart endianness versus shared boot artifacts.

A.summary: The overall direction is reasonable, but the series still mixes per-hart endianness with system-wide/shared boot data assumptions.

B.summary: Patch application/build were not validated here; the provided metadata already shows `git am` failure and `BUILD STATUS: not_run`.

C.summary: Two concrete correctness issues were found: BE ELF kernels are still loaded as LSB, and page-table-walk endianness is derived from current privilege rather than the stage-specific status bits required by the spec.

D.summary: The added test coverage appears too narrow for this feature set; it does not cover MMU enable paths under differing privilege/stage endianness or mixed-hart configurations.

E.summary: Not merge-ready until the blocking correctness issues are fixed and the shared-boot/per-hart endianness model is clarified or constrained.

findings.title: BE kernel ELF loading is still hard-wired to little-endian
findings.description: The commit message says ELF loading should select `ELFDATA2MSB` for big-endian harts, but the actual diff still passes `ELFDATA2LSB` unconditionally. That means a big-endian kernel image will be parsed with the wrong ELF data encoding and either fail to load or load incorrectly. This is a direct boot-path blocker for the feature the series is adding.
findings.suggestion: Change the loader call to use the hart/boot-info endianness, e.g. `info->is_big_endian ? ELFDATA2MSB : ELFDATA2LSB`, and add a functional test that boots a BE ELF kernel image rather than relying only on later runtime behavior.
findings.confidence_reason: The diff shown in patch 4 keeps `ELFDATA2LSB` unchanged despite the changelog explicitly claiming otherwise.

findings.title: Runtime endianness helper uses current privilege, which is not sufficient for page-table walks
findings.description: `riscv_cpu_data_is_big_endian()` keys off `env->priv` and is then reused for page-table-walk accesses. For implicit translation-structure accesses, the privileged spec requires stage-specific endianness control, not simply the current privilege's data endianness. In particular, supervisor-managed page tables are controlled by `SBE` (and guest stage by the corresponding virtualized setting), so using `MBE` whenever the CPU is currently in M-mode can decode PTEs with the wrong byte order during translation-related paths.
findings.suggestion: Do not reuse the generic current-privilege helper for translation structures. Introduce a dedicated walker-endianness helper that selects the bit mandated by the active translation stage (`SBE`/virtualized equivalent, and the right rule for G-stage) and use that consistently for both PTE reads and A/D updates.
findings.confidence_reason: The helper semantics are explicit in the diff, and the new page-table-walk code directly consumes it. The spec requirement for translation-structure endianness is stage-specific, not a simple `env->priv` mapping.

findings.title: Boot code silently assumes all harts share hart0 endianness
findings.description: The new helper returns `harts->harts[0].cfg.big_endian` and that single value drives shared boot artifacts such as reset-vector data words and firmware dynamic info. However, patch 1 introduces the property as a per-CPU/per-hart setting. As written, a machine can be configured with mixed-endian harts, but the boot ROM payload is serialized in only hart0's endianness, which is inherently wrong for the other harts. If mixed-endian harts are unsupported, the series needs to reject that configuration explicitly.
findings.suggestion: Either validate at machine/hart-array creation that all harts have the same endianness and reject mixed configurations, or rework the boot path so shared artifacts are not derived from hart0's setting while still advertising a per-hart property.
findings.confidence_reason: The helper is shown in the diff and all boot-time consumers use that single shared value, while the property itself is added on `RISCVCPU`.

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
